audit_trail.notes: Mechanical average scores declined from cycle 1 (5.43) to cycle 2 (4.67) as reviewers incorporated each other's critiques in the cross-review phase. The most consequential new finding in cycle 2 was codex's identification of Liu-Lyu-Raju JHEP 2021 (Ref [22]) as directly prior art that undermines the 'first identification' claim more severely than the authors' own Ref [33]. The cusp-vs-extremum inconsistency in the Kasner crossover line definition was flagged independently by all three reviewers in cycle 2. Codex resolved gemini's parameter-fixing objection (L=1 vs. varying P) as a rendering misread after checking the PDF. The HTML bundle omits equation content due to arXiv rendering limitations; some objections in cycle 1 related to unreadable equations were appropriately downgraded in cycle 2 after PDF inspection. Reviewer confidence is moderate to moderately high (0.65–0.85); this is a specialized subfield and several key questions require expert reading of the cited preprints and the full PDF.

general_reader_takeaway: This paper tries to show that the geometry deep inside a black hole—specifically near its singularity—changes character when the black hole undergoes a thermodynamic phase transition, and can serve as a novel diagnostic for that transition. The review board found the core numerical observation (two branches of the transition produce qualitatively different interior behavior) plausible and interesting. However, the paper's boldest claims—that this constitutes an "independent" probe and a "first identification"—do not survive scrutiny: earlier published work appears to have already studied singularity geometry across phase transitions, and the interior is mathematically fixed by exterior data rather than being truly independent. The most important caveat is an internally inconsistent definition of the paper's main new concept, the "Kasner crossover line."
human_spotchecks: Inspect Fig. 2 (bottom panel) with raw numerical data or a high-resolution version of the PDF: is the supercritical feature in p_t(T) a smooth extremum (consistent with 'crossover' language) or a non-analytic cusp with a discontinuous first derivative (consistent with 'abrupt change point' language)? The answer fundamentally changes whether the 'Kasner crossover line' concept is physically coherent.

Read Liu, Lyu, and Raju JHEP 10, 140 (2021) [Ref 22] directly: does that paper establish that Kasner exponents diagnose first-order phase transitions in holographic black holes, and if so, how does the present paper's scalarized charged-scalar model and supercritical-line contribution differ from that earlier result?

Read Zhao et al. arXiv:2512.24893 [Ref 33] directly: does that preprint frame the Kasner exponent as a probe of phase transitions (in which case the priority claim is undermined), or does it study oscillatory properties near a critical point without that framing?

Verify Eq. (23) in the PDF against standard references for holographic thermodynamics in the canonical (fixed-charge) ensemble: are the Hawking-Ross boundary terms appropriate for fixed charge density included, or are grand-canonical boundary terms used inconsistently?

Assess whether the physical mechanism connecting the exterior thermodynamic branch to the interior oscillatory-vs-smooth distinction is explained anywhere in the PDF (e.g., via the scalar field amplitude at the horizon setting the number of Josephson oscillations), or whether only numerical demonstration is provided.
public_summary:
The paper argues that the geometry near a black hole's central singularity carries a imprint of whether the black hole is in one thermodynamic phase or another, specifically for a class of Anti-de Sitter black holes decorated with a charged scalar field. By numerically following the equations of motion from the black hole horizon inward, the authors compute a quantity called the Kasner exponent and show it behaves very differently on the two sides of a first-order phase transition: wildly oscillating with temperature on one branch, smoothly varying on the other. They further claim that in the "supercritical" regime above the critical point, a distinct feature of this exponent defines a new type of crossover line not captured by standard thermodynamic methods.

The review board found the branch-dependent qualitative difference in interior scalar field profiles to be the paper's most concrete and credible result, consistent with established physics of Josephson oscillations inside holographic black holes. However, sustained objections undercut the paper's principal novelty and significance claims. Prior work—particularly Liu, Lyu, and Raju (JHEP 2021, Ref [22])—is explicitly titled "Black hole singularities across phase transitions" and appears to have already demonstrated Kasner-exponent behavior across phase transitions; the paper does not engage with this prior result. The authors' own earlier preprint (Ref [33]) also probed Kasner oscillations near a critical point. The paper's framing of the singularity as an "independent" probe is conceptually circular: in classical holography, integrating from the horizon inward is deterministic, so any exterior discontinuity automatically propagates to the interior. Most concretely, the paper's flagship new concept—the "Kasner crossover line"—is defined three different ways across the text (smooth extremum in the abstract, "abrupt change point" in the main body, "abrupt slope change" in the caption), and these are not equivalent; if the feature is non-analytic, it represents an interior phase transition rather than a thermodynamic crossover, fundamentally changing the interpretation. Thermodynamic terminology (calling a canonical-ensemble potential the "Gibbs free energy") and the absence of numerical convergence details also drew criticism from multiple reviewers.
reviewer_disagreements: Verdict: codex recommended 'contested' in both cycles on grounds that the numerical methodology is too under-specified for the results to be considered settled; claude and gemini recommended 'routine,' judging that the core numerical observation is plausible but the framing requires conceptual revision rather than a fundamental dispute about results.

Weight of the prior-art problem: codex (cycle 2) identified Liu-Lyu-Raju JHEP 2021 as the most directly damaging prior work and elevated this to a major severity objection that would affect the verdict; claude and gemini focused primarily on the authors' own Ref [33] and treated the priority issue as moderate rather than fatal.

Novelty scores diverged sharply in cycle 1 (codex: 7.0, gemini: 6.0, claude: 5.0) before converging downward in cycle 2 (codex: 4.5, gemini: 4.0, claude: 5.0) after cross-review incorporated the prior-art concern.

'Independent probe' framing: gemini maintained that this is circular and moderately severe; codex agreed but added a nuance—determinism does not by itself explain the oscillatory-vs-smooth contrast, which is a non-trivial numerical result even if not independent in a strict causal sense.

Parameter fixing inconsistency (L=1 vs. varying P): gemini flagged this as a minor objection in cycle 1; codex checked the PDF and resolved it as a non-issue—the paper fixes q=1, r_h=1, G_N=1 and reports specific P values, which is standard in extended-phase-space thermodynamics.
Severity of HTML equation omission: claude noted it as a minor limitation preventing audit; gemini dismissed it as a rendering artifact rather than a paper flaw.

strongest_objections.objection: The paper's central priority claim—'the first identification of the black hole singularity as a new class of probe for phase transitions'—appears directly contradicted by Liu, Lyu, and Raju JHEP 2021 (Ref [22]), whose title is 'Black hole singularities across phase transitions' and which studies Kasner-exponent behavior across first- and second-order transitions. The paper cites this work without explaining how the present contribution differs. The authors' own Ref [33] further weakens the claim by already studying p_t oscillations near critical points.

The paper's key new concept, the 'Kasner crossover line,' is defined inconsistently across sections: the abstract and conclusion call it a 'distinct extremum' of p_t(T); the main text calls it an 'abrupt change point'; the Fig. 3 caption calls it a point where 'the slope of p_t changes abruptly.' A smooth extremum and a non-analytic cusp are mathematically distinct objects that would generally yield different loci, and if the feature is a cusp it signals an interior dynamical phase transition (discrete change in Josephson oscillation count) rather than the smooth thermodynamic crossover the label implies.
strongest_objections.paper_location: Abstract; Fig. 2 bottom panel; Fig. 3 caption; Conclusion

strongest_objections.objection: The numerical procedure for extracting the Kasner exponent p_t is not specified in sufficient detail: the fitting window near the singularity, numerical resolution, convergence tests, error estimates, handling of Josephson oscillations, and treatment of Kasner inversions are all absent. Without these, the oscillatory-vs-smooth distinction cannot be independently reproduced or assessed for robustness.
strongest_objections.paper_location: Section 'Interior solutions of black holes and Kasner geometry'; equations (24)-(27); Fig. 2

strongest_objections.objection: The claim that the interior geometry constitutes an 'independent probe' of the phase transition is conceptually overblown. In classical holography, the interior is found by integrating the equations of motion inward from the horizon; because a first-order transition causes a discontinuous jump in horizon data, any interior quantity including p_t must jump as well—trivially and necessarily, not independently. The interior cannot determine which branch minimizes the free energy; exterior thermodynamics remains required.

The paper states it works in the canonical ensemble (fixed charge density) but labels the resulting thermodynamic potential the 'Gibbs free energy.' The canonical-ensemble potential is a Helmholtz-type free energy, not the Gibbs free energy. Whether the Hawking-Ross boundary terms appropriate for the fixed-charge ensemble are correctly included in Eq. (23) is not shown.

strongest_supported_claims: The interior scalar field profile on the high-temperature scalarized branch exhibits oscillatory approach to the singularity, while the low-temperature branch produces a smooth, monotone profile—a qualitative contrast consistent with differing Josephson oscillation counts set by the horizon scalar field amplitude on each branch (Fig. 1 lower panel).

The near-singularity Kasner exponent p_t varies qualitatively differently as a function of temperature on the two stable branches of the first-order transition: oscillatory on Branch 1 and smooth on Branch 2 (Fig. 2 top panel).

The framework of universal Kasner epochs characterizing the near-singularity geometry of scalarized AdS black holes, following Hartnoll et al. 2021 (Ref [17]) and subsequent references, is well-established background on which the paper's numerics correctly build.
technical_takeaway: The paper numerically integrates the interior equations of motion for scalarized AdS black holes (charged scalar with a quartic nonlinear coupling that induces a first-order transition) from the horizon to the singularity, extracting the Kasner exponent p_t that characterizes near-singularity geometry. The paper claims: (1) p_t is oscillatory in temperature on the high-T scalarized branch and smooth on the low-T branch, converging near the critical point; (2) in the supercritical region a feature in p_t(T) defines a "Kasner crossover line" independent of Widom and Frenkel lines. The core oscillatory-vs-smooth contrast survived cross-review as a plausible numerical result, supported by the expected difference in Josephson oscillation counts between branches. Three objections were sustained across both review cycles: the "first identification" priority claim is contradicted by Liu-Lyu-Raju JHEP 2021 (Ref [22]) and the authors' own Ref [33]; the "Kasner crossover line" is defined inconsistently as both a smooth extremum and a non-analytic cusp, which are physically distinct (the latter would signal an interior dynamical phase transition, not a crossover); and the "independent probe" framing is conceptually overblown because the interior is deterministically fixed by horizon data via ODE integration.
